title: Find second smallest element
description: Given an array find the second smallest element

examples.explanation: Step 1: Sort array → [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]. Step 2: firstUnique = 1. Step 3: Iterate i=1: arr[1]=1 equals firstUnique, continue. i=2: arr[2]=3 differs from firstUnique, return 3.

Step 1: Sort array → [7, 7, 7, 7]. Step 2: firstUnique = 7. Step 3: Iterate i=1: arr[1]=7 equals firstUnique, continue. i=2: arr[2]=7 equals, continue. i=3: arr[3]=7 equals, continue. Loop ends, no second unique element found, return None.

approaches.name: Brute Force via Sorting O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the array in ascending order and return the second element, handling duplicates by skipping the first unique element. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) depending on sorting algorithm

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force via Sorting O(n log n)
ladders.desc: Complete solution: sort array and return second smallest unique element.
ladders.explanation:
This approach solves the problem by first sorting the array in ascending order, then scanning from the beginning to find the second distinct element. The intuition is that after sorting, the smallest elements appear first, making it trivial to identify the second smallest by simply looking at the second position. However, we must handle duplicates carefully—if the first element repeats, we need to skip all its occurrences to find the next distinct element. The algorithm works
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort array → [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]. Step 2: firstUnique = 1. Step 3: Iterate i=1: arr[1]=1 equals firstUnique, continue. i=2: arr[2]=3 differs from firstUnique, return 3.

Step 1: Sort array → [7, 7, 7, 7]. Step 2: firstUnique = 7. Step 3: Iterate i=1: arr[1]=7 equals firstUnique, continue. i=2: arr[2]=7 equals, continue. i=3: arr[3]=7 equals, continue. Loop ends, no second unique element found, return None.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sorting the Array
ladders.desc: Sort array in ascending order as helper for main problem.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine focuses on sorting the input array, which is the most critical step in the brute-force approach. The intuition is that by arranging elements in non-decreasing order, we transform the problem of finding the second smallest into a simple linear scan, as the smallest values congregate at the start. We can use any comparison-based sorting algorithm; for simplicity, we assume an efficient O(n log n) sort like quicksort or mergesort. Step-by-step: 1. Take an unsort
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Input [3,1,4,1,5]. Step 2: Apply sort (e.g., quicksort): partition and recursively sort to get [1,1,3,4,5]. Step 3: Output sorted array.
Step 1: Input [7,7,7,7]. Step 2: Sort (already sorted, no changes). Step 3: Output same array.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Linear Scan for Second Distinct Element
ladders.desc: Scan sorted array to find first element different from the first.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic involves traversing the sorted array to identify the second smallest unique element after sorting. The intuition is that in a sorted array, all occurrences of the smallest element are at the beginning, so by skipping these and taking the next distinct value, we directly obtain the second smallest. This works because sorting ensures monotonic order, making the scan efficient and straightforward. Step-by-step: 1. Assume input is a sorted array in non-decreasing 
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Input sorted array. Step 2: len=5 ≥2, proceed. Step 3: firstUnique = 1. Step 4: Iterate: i=1: 1 equals first, continue; i=2: 3 differs, return 3.
Step 1: Input sorted array. Step 2: len=4 ≥2, proceed. Step 3: firstUnique = 7. Step 4: Iterate: i=1: 7 equals, continue; i=2: 7 equals, continue; i=3: 7 equals, continue. Loop ends, return None.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Elements for Uniqueness
ladders.desc: Determine if two elements are different to identify distinct values.
ladders.explanation:
This building block involves comparing two elements to check if they are distinct, which is essential for skipping duplicates in the scan. The intuition is that in a sorted array, consecutive identical values represent duplicates of the same smallest element, so by comparing each element to the first unique one, we can detect when a new value appears. This works because equality comparison is straightforward and allows us to filter out repetitions. Step-by-step: 1. Take two v
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Input a=1, b=3. Step 2: Compare 1 != 3. Step 3: Result True (distinct).
Step 1: Input a=7, b=7. Step 2: Compare 7 != 7. Step 3: Result False (not distinct).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its position.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves accessing elements in an array by their index, which is fundamental for traversing and manipulating data in the algorithm. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, allowing direct access via indices starting from 0. This works because indexing provides constant-time lookup, enabling efficient scanning. Step-by-step: 1. Have an array and an integer index i. 2. Check that i is within bounds (0 ≤ i < array length).
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Input arr=[3,1,4], i=0. Step 2: Check bounds: 0 in [0,3), valid. Step 3: Return arr[0] = 3.
Step 1: Input arr=[7,7,7,7], i=2. Step 2: Check bounds: 2 in [0,4), valid. Step 3: Return arr[2] = 7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Arrays store multiple values accessible by index starting from 0.
ladders.explanation: An array is an ordered collection where each element is stored at a specific index (starting from 0). This allows systematic access and modification of elements — essential for finding the second smallest element in an array.

examples.trace: Array has 5 elements. Indices: 0,1,2,3,4. arr[2] = 4.

approaches.name: Linear Scan with Two Variables O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array once, maintaining the smallest and second smallest elements seen so far, updating them as needed. This avoids sorting and is efficient for any input size.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Linear Scan with Two Variables O(n)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding the second smallest element in an array using a single pass with two tracking variables.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires finding the second smallest element in an array without sorting. The linear scan approach is optimal because it processes each element exactly once, achieving O(n) time complexity with O(1) extra space. The intuition is to maintain two variables: 'smallest' tracks the absolute minimum seen so far, and 'second_smallest' tracks the smallest element that's greater than 'smallest'. As we iterate through the array, we compare each element with these two track
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Start: smallest=inf, second_smallest=inf
Element 5: 5 < inf → second_smallest=inf, smallest=5
Element 2: 2 < 5 → second_smallest=5, smallest=2
Element 8: 8 not < 2, 8 < 5? No → no update
Element 1: 1 < 2 → second_smallest=2, smallest=1
Element 9: 9 not < 1, 9 < 2? No → no update
End: smallest=1, second_smallest=2 → return 2

Start: smallest=inf, second_smallest=inf
Element 3: 3 < inf → second_smallest=inf, smallest=3
Element 3: 3 not < 3, 3 < inf? Yes but 3 == smallest → no update (skip duplicate)
Element 3: same as above → no update
Element 1: 1 < 3 → second_smallest=3, smallest=1
Element 2: 2 not < 1, 2 < 3 and 2 != 1 → second_smallest=2
End: smallest=1, second_smallest=2 → return 2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Update Two Minimum Trackers
ladders.desc: Implement the core update logic that maintains smallest and second smallest values during iteration.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the linear scan approach. It defines how to update two variables ('smallest' and 'second_smallest') when processing a new element from an array. The intuition is that we want to keep track of the absolute minimum and the next minimum (second smallest) as we see elements. When we encounter a new element, we compare it with our current trackers. If it's smaller than our current smallest, it becomes the new smallest, and the old smallest becomes 
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Start: smallest=5, second_smallest=10, num=3
Check: 3 < 5? Yes → update: second_smallest becomes 5, smallest becomes 3
End: smallest=3, second_smallest=5

Start: smallest=2, second_smallest=7, num=5
Check: 5 < 2? No → move to else if
Check: 5 < 7 and 5 != 2? Yes → update: second_smallest becomes 5
End: smallest=2, second_smallest=5

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Two Minimums in a Sequence
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the technique of tracking the smallest and second smallest values while iterating through elements.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic involves the fundamental technique of maintaining two minimum values (smallest and second smallest) as you process a sequence of numbers. The intuition is that by keeping these two values updated, you can always know the top two smallest elements without needing to store or sort the entire sequence. This is efficient because it requires only O(1) extra space and O(n) time. The key insight is that when you see a new element, it can affect either the smallest, t
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Start: min1=inf, min2=inf
x=4: 4 < inf → min2=inf, min1=4
x=2: 2 < 4 → min2=4, min1=2
x=7: 7 not < 2, 7 < 4? No → no update
x=1: 1 < 2 → min2=2, min1=1
End: min1=1, min2=2

Start: min1=inf, min2=inf
x=5: 5 < inf → min2=inf, min1=5
x=5: 5 not < 5, 5 < inf? Yes but 5 == min1 → no update
x=3: 3 < 5 → min2=5, min1=3
x=6: 6 not < 3, 6 < 5? No → no update
End: min1=3, min2=5

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing and Updating Variables in a Loop
ladders.desc: Practice iterating through an array and updating variables based on comparisons with current values.
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on the fundamental skill of iterating through a sequence (like an array) and updating one or more variables based on comparisons with each element. The intuition is that many algorithms involve processing elements one by one and maintaining some state (like minimum, maximum, sum, etc.) that gets updated as new information arrives. Here, we specifically practice updating two variables that represent extreme values (minima). The key concept is that d
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Start: min_val = 3 (first element)
Index 1: arr[1]=7, 7 < 3? No → min_val stays 3
Index 2: arr[2]=2, 2 < 3? Yes → min_val becomes 2
Index 3: arr[3]=9, 9 < 2? No → min_val stays 2
End: min_val=2

Start: min_val = 10
Index 1: arr[1]=5, 5 < 10? Yes → min_val becomes 5
Index 2: arr[2]=8, 8 < 5? No → min_val stays 5
Index 3: arr[3]=5, 5 < 5? No → min_val stays 5
End: min_val=5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Numbers and Updating a Variable
ladders.desc: Learn how to compare two numbers and conditionally update a variable based on the result.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation is a fundamental programming concept: comparing two values and updating a variable if a condition is met. The intuition is that in algorithms, we often need to check relationships between numbers (like less than, greater than) and take action accordingly, such as assigning a new value to a variable. This is the building block for more complex logic like finding minimums or maximums. The operation involves: 1. Having two numbers: a current value stored in 
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Start: current_min=5, new_num=3
Compare: 3 < 5? Yes → update current_min to 3
End: current_min=3

Start: current_min=2, new_num=7
Compare: 7 < 2? No → no update, current_min stays 2
End: current_min=2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Arrays store multiple values accessible by index starting from 0.
ladders.explanation: An array is an ordered collection where each element is stored at a specific index (starting from 0). This allows systematic access and modification of elements — essential for finding the second smallest element in an array.

examples.trace: Array has 5 elements. Indices: 0,1,2,3,4. arr[2] = 4.

approaches.name: Heap-based Selection O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a min-heap to efficiently find the second smallest by popping the smallest element first, then the next. This is useful if multiple queries are needed or for streaming data.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n + k log n) where k=2, typically O(n)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Heap-based Selection O(n)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using heap-based selection to find the second smallest element in an array.
ladders.explanation:
The heap-based approach leverages the properties of a min-heap to efficiently find the second smallest element. A min-heap is a complete binary tree where each node's value is less than or equal to its children's values, ensuring the smallest element is always at the root. The intuition is to build a min-heap from the array, which organizes elements so the smallest is readily accessible. By popping (removing) the root twice, we first get the smallest element, then the second 
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Build min-heap from [3, 1, 4, 1, 5] -> heap becomes [1, 1, 4, 3, 5] (smallest at root). Step 2: Pop root (1) -> heap becomes [1, 3, 4, 5]. Step 3: Pop root again (1) -> returns 1 as second smallest.
Step 1: Build min-heap from [7, 2, 8, 5] -> heap becomes [2, 5, 8, 7]. Step 2: Pop root (2) -> heap becomes [5, 7, 8]. Step 3: Pop root (5) -> returns 5 as second smallest.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pop Smallest Element from Min-Heap
ladders.desc: Extract and return the smallest element from a min-heap, maintaining heap properties.
ladders.explanation:
Popping from a min-heap is crucial because it allows sequential access to the smallest elements. In a min-heap, the smallest element is always at the root (index 0). The intuition is to remove this root and reorganize the heap to preserve the min-heap property. The algorithm: 1) Store the root value to return later. 2) Replace the root with the last element in the heap. 3) Remove the last element (reducing heap size). 4) 'Heapify down' from the root: compare the new root with
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Root is 1, store it. Step 2: Replace root with last element 4 -> [4, 3, 2, 5]. Step 3: Remove last -> [4, 3, 2, 5] (size 4). Step 4: Heapify down: compare 4 with children 3 and 2, swap with smaller child 2 -> [2, 3, 4, 5]. Compare 4 with children (none), stop. Return 1.

Step 1: Root is 2, store it. Step 2: Replace root with last element 6 -> [6, 5, 3, 7]. Step 3: Remove last -> [6, 5, 3, 7] (size 4). Step 4: Heapify down: compare 6 with children 5 and 3, swap with smaller child 3 -> [3, 5, 6, 7]. Compare 6 with children (none), stop. Return 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Heapify Down to Maintain Min-Heap Property
ladders.desc: Restore min-heap property by moving an element down the heap, swapping with smaller children.
ladders.explanation:
Heapify down is essential after removing the root to keep the heap valid. The intuition is that when a larger element is placed at the root, it must sink down to its correct position by repeatedly swapping with its smaller child. This ensures the parent is always less than or equal to children. The algorithm: 1) Start at a given index (usually root after pop). 2) While the node has children: a) Find the smallest child. b) If the node is greater than the smallest child, swap t
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: At index 0, value 4. Children: left (index 1, value 3), right (index 2, value 2). Smallest child is 2 at index 2. Step 2: Swap 4 and 2 -> [2, 3, 4, 5]. Step 3: Move to index 2, value 4. No children, stop.
Step 1: At index 0, value 6. Children: left 5 (index 1), right 3 (index 2). Smallest child is 3 at index 2. Step 2: Swap 6 and 3 -> [3, 5, 6, 7]. Step 3: Move to index 2, value 6. No children, stop.

ladders.title: Building Block — Build Min-Heap from Array
ladders.desc: Transform an unsorted array into a min-heap using heapify operations.
ladders.explanation:
Building a min-heap is necessary to initialize the data structure for efficient queries. The intuition is to start from the last non-leaf node and apply heapify down backwards to the root, ensuring each subtree satisfies the min-heap property. This bottom-up approach is efficient because heapify down works in O(log n) but the total time is O(n) due to the structure of the heap. The algorithm: 1) Start from the last non-leaf index (floor(n/2)-1). 2) For each index from that po
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: n=5, last non-leaf index = floor(5/2)-1 = 1. Step 2: Start at i=1 (value 1): heapify down on [3,1,4,1,5] at index 1 -> no swap (1 is smaller than children 3 and 5). Step 3: i=0 (value 3): heapify down: compare with children 1 and 4, swap with smaller child 1 -> [1,3,4,1,5]. Now at index 1 (value 3), compare with children 1 and 5, swap with 1 -> [1,1,4,3,5]. Final heap.

Step 1: n=4, last non-leaf index = floor(4/2)-1 = 1. Step 2: i=1 (value 2): heapify down on [7,2,8,5] at index 1 -> no swap (2 is smaller than child 5). Step 3: i=0 (value 7): heapify down: compare with children 2 and 8, swap with 2 -> [2,7,8,5]. Now at index 1 (value 7), compare with child 5, swap -> [2,5,8,7]. Final heap.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Swap Elements in Array
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation to compare two elements and swap them if needed, used in heap maintenance.
ladders.explanation:
Comparing and swapping is a core operation in heap algorithms to maintain order. The intuition is that by comparing values, we can determine their relative order and swap to position smaller elements higher in the heap. This is done repeatedly in heapify down to move elements to correct positions. The algorithm: 1) Compare two elements at given indices. 2) If the first is greater than the second (for min-heap), swap their values. 3) Swapping involves storing one value tempora
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare arr[0]=3 and arr[1]=1. Since 3 > 1, swap. Step 2: temp = 3, arr[0] = 1, arr[1] = 3. Result: [1, 3].
Step 1: Compare arr[0]=5 and arr[2]=8. Since 5 <= 8, no swap. Result: [5, 2, 8].
